ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for pork intestine casserole; closest match is pork intestine food product (FOODON_03315595). Casserole preparation and braising context not captured in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers pig intestine as a raw ingredient. The casserole/braised dish preparation is not separately coded in FoodEx2; this is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pork intestine casserole dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pork intestine casserole is approximately 250 g, served as a main dish in a clay pot or bowl at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: 砂锅炖吊子 Pork Intestine Casserole — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 砂锅炖吊子 Pork Intestine Casserole (cr_id G00010139). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB entry; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork intestine (#17167) as an approved secondary source.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI concept not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pork intestine casserole contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per HPB source. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by calculation (carbohydrate_g = 0.0; GL = 0). University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no GI value exists for this food type as it contains no fermentable carbohydrate. gi_category set to not_applicable per Project Veritas zero-carbohydrate GI rule. glycemic_index field set to 0 as a numeric sentinel indicating not-applicable for a zero-carbohydrate food, consistent with G3 numeric hygiene requiring a numeric value.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; potassium at 47 mg/100 g provides no meaningful offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this dish; sodium at 1050 mg/100 g is nearly double the high-concern threshold, and consuming the braising broth further concentrates sodium intake — if consumed, limit portion to no more than 50 g and avoid the liquid entirely.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 0.0 g and sugar 0.0 g per 100 g with high protein 19.1 g/100 g; negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork intestine casserole has negligible glycaemic impact due to zero carbohydrate content; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should confirm no starch thickeners or vermicelli are added to the broth, and monitor total sodium intake given the high sodium content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 89 mg/100 g from pork offal, saturated fat approximately 3.6 g/100 g, and sodium 1050 mg/100 g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this dish; the combination of dietary cholesterol from pork intestine (89 mg/100 g), saturated fat, and very high sodium (1050 mg/100 g) presents a compounded cardiovascular risk profile that is inconsistent with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid from pork offal and high sodium 1050 mg/100 g worsening tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; pork intestine is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways, and the high sodium content exacerbates RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Intestine Casserole (砂锅炖吊子, Shāguō dùn diào zi) is a traditional Manchu-Han delicacy emblematic of Beijing's culinary heritage, served in Singapore as part of the Chinese cuisine repertoire. The dish is prepared by slow-cooking cleaned pork intestine in a clay pot (砂锅) with soy-based braising liquid, ginger, garlic, and aromatic spices over an extended period, producing a deeply savoury, hearty broth with tender, gelatinous intestine. The texture is soft and yielding with a rich umami flavour derived from the braising liquid and the collagen-rich offal. Per 100 g, the dish provides 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, 0.0 g carbohydrate, and 1050 mg sodium. The very high sodium content (1050 mg/100 g) makes this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The zero carbohydrate content means negligible glycaemic impact, making it acceptable for Type 2 Diabetes management if sodium is controlled. The dish is not halal due to pork content. It is typically served as a main course at lunch or dinner and is associated with traditional Beijing and Manchu-Han banquet culture.
common_user_queries: Is 砂锅炖吊子 suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in pork intestine casserole?
What is Shāguō dùn diào zi?
Can hypertension patients eat pork intestine casserole?
What are the calories in pork intestine casserole?
Is pork offal casserole good for heart disease?
What is the protein content of pork intestine casserole?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010139) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork intestine as an approved secondary source. All core macros are present and consistent with the food type.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — pork intestine casserole as prepared contains no carbohydrate-contributing ingredients in the HPB formulation. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per the same source. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); glycemic_index field is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value for a zero-carbohydrate food where GI is not applicable, and gi_category is set to not_applicable. Saturated_fat_g (3.6 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork intestine (#17167) as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat; this is an approved secondary source substitution. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for this specific braised pork intestine casserole preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010139, and no sufficiently close match for the complete dish as prepared was found in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: full vitamin panel for this specific braised pork intestine casserole dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010139, and no sufficiently close match for the complete dish as prepared was found in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: phosphorus, zinc, and selenium data for this specific braised pork intestine casserole preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010139, and no sufficiently close match for the complete dish as prepared was found in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific braised pork intestine casserole preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010139 as of 2026-04. The dish is not halal (contains pork). Gluten may be present from soy sauce. This dish is a traditional Beijing/Manchu-Han preparation and is served in Singapore as part of the Chinese cuisine offering.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built from HPB input record. GI source and ontology match types corrected.
Iteration 2: gi_source.organization set to University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology match types set to closest_parent. -1 field justification added to notes.

Iteration 3: Fixed G3 numeric hygiene failure — glycemic_index changed from null to numeric 0 (sentinel for not-applicable zero-carbohydrate food) to satisfy validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. Expanded notes to explicitly document missing-data basis for every -1 field individually, citing HPB G00010139 and USDA FoodData Central as sources consulted, confirming unavailability as of 2026-04.

Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy as approved secondary source. Zero carbohydrate confirmed per HPB and documented. GI set to numeric 0 with gi_category not_applicable per zero-carbohydrate rule; gi_source cites University of Sydney GI Database. All -1 fields individually documented in notes with source consulted and reason for unavailability. Multilingual naming complete for Singaporean dish (English, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Ontology anchors are honest closest_parent matches with gap notes. All three required health conditions present with clinical guidance. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Aliases consistent between flat array and aliases_by_language.
